author: @nixonsmateruby1
text: As you said, laws are there to be broken, so if you ignore the laws of thermodynamics and see it as it is ONE LAW, then heating becomes more achievable. There is only one law of Thermo dynamic and it's motion, without motion there is no heat, so you "slow" the motion to hold on to the heat,  and "speed"  the motion to create the heat. So if you think you can't heat a room with a tealight, well that's wrong, because if you "slow" the movement of the heat you can get more heat. I can prove it by getting a water heater and put a tealight in it, and if your water heater is insulated and the heat leaves slowly, and it's exit bypassed a rock, or something that retains the heat longer, then you get the heat of the tealight in slow motion, and it's hotter. I can stick a thermometer in that heater and it's 80°c, so if I add a coiled chimney that candle heat has to wait to escape and it gets warmer. So if that water heater holds 20 litres, and it's 80c and a granite slab is soaking that heat up, then long after that tealight goes out you have residual heat that can't leave as it's been made to move in slow motion. Now add another 3 tealights and you get more heat. So, if you then have a pot of water, it will warm up and you could put that in a kettle and heat a cuppa up in half the cost.

author: @shortbuslife3440
text:
I get what your saying about mass heaters Rob but you are missing one part about them, that is the heat that would normally go up the flu pipe is extracted in to the mass so less of the heat goes outside, the barrel is where most of the burn actually takes place because of the secondary air intake and then radiates the heat, this is one of the reasons why at the moment you can't install them in a house as the flu temperature is to low for HETAS regulations.
I'll also add that because of the secondary burn this produces less soot and if you add a bell/stratification chamber this will capture most of the soot or carbon which you could then use for some of your projects or can be cleaned out once every few years.

author: @Adam.Rushing
text:
Excellent points sir, on par.  The only 2 things I would add.

1) Every time you convert an energy source, you lose some, because some energy is consumed making the conversion.   

So for instance, if the power plant where you live uses diesel to spin their generators that power the grid, even if your electric heating unit is "100% efficient", it will still be less effective than if you used the same diesel to heat your home.  This is because the energy went from stored/chemical energy (diesel) to heat energy (turbine combustion) to mechanical energy (turbine/gearbox/generator rotation) to electrical energy.  At each step along the way, some energy was consumed to the process, i.e. friction in the bearings, and line loss in the electrical lines.  This means that, all other things being equal, it will almost always be cheaper to use the same diesel to heat your home. 

2)  The smaller the system, the more efficient it is.  This means there is less waste when you to produce your own heat rather than relying on huge industrial machines to heat enough for everyone in the city.

author: @Paxmax
text: I think the dehumidifier option that raises many questions. Humans are (somewhat) moist creatures. Lowering humidity increases effectiveness of sweat and moisture loss, so you are effectively more cooled. So, it feels a bit of double edged sword I'd think carefully about b4 implementing. Sure, if your home is like a damp cave you'll need to dehumidify for other reasons that heat. I usually find most winters pretty dry indoors, often hanging around the 30's% RH. Trying to get moisture out of 30% RH air will require silica gel or other similarly functioning chemical. Running an electric heat pump condensation unit will not much, if anything at all. I have a hefty (600+W compressor/condensation) dryer for laundry and it really start to struggle when it is below 50%RH, I don't think I'll capture squat under 40%RH. Even if it works, I'm failing to see any economy in lowerering humidity, chemicals cost (or regen the chemicals cost), in my case, electricity cost.

author: @tonystone3397
text: Great stuff as always Rob, you are doing great work helping people keep warm. I would like to make a point about getting the most out of a given amount of energy. In my front room we have a back boiler, in the kitchen we have a Stanley range with a u shape boiler. Three stone of seasoned fire wood will heat the front room and give us a full tank of hot water. The same in the range will run 11 radiators, heat a full tank of water and last at least 10hrs, and the house will stay warm for 24hrs, the house is well insulated walls pumped, attic sprayed with foam. Thanks again Rob, I am learning so much here, keep up the good work.

author: @TheDickPuller
text:
Thanks Robert, as a Plumber/Heating Engineer for nearly 50 years, it never fails to surprise me how stupid people are when it comes to Central Heating.  I’ve had a lifetime of explaining things like Thermostats.........But the biggest vacant stare comes from explaining a standard Radiator & the type of heat it gives off.  It’s the bit; ‘it mostly gives off convection heat & your ceiling is the hottest part of the room, that one, literally, goes over their head!!
The big one is explaining Low Temperature Heating, they hang onto the notion of the ‘hearth effect’, where they can’t feel warm unless they feel radiant heat on their arse.
Their radiator can be 50°C & the room at 21°C, but they’re still cold & moaning.

We now live in a world of people wearing very little clothes in their homes & they expect CH to be cheap to run.

The concept of Low Temperature Central Heating is very simple, double the size of Radiators & turn the heating appliance(Heat Pump or Condensing Boiler) temperature down.

Also, moist air indoors has a much higher effect on the cost to heat your home, more than what you stated.
Thanks again mate, great video as always.

author: @mollymaccorkle7054
text:
Is isn't that hot goes to cold, it is that hot and cold try to reach equilibrium.
Insulation slows that down..
Hot, as such, expands, cold contract, that causes drafts, so that putting an air inlet to a air tight stove outside, creates a warmer house.
Photons, on the other hand are nothing until they "hit" something. A better way of say it is that they excite something. If that was not true, the snow closer to the sun, like on a mountain would melt first, but the air is thinner, so molecules are not as close together, making for less friction. The movement of the molecules makes friction, giving it a charge. Friction creates magnetism, magnetism creates friction, friction creates charge, charge creates magnetism.
Think of magnets on a flywheel, moving past a magneto, that creates the spark for the internal combustion. The movement of the molecule in the layers of the magneto create the friction. Stored energy in the battery starts the movement, in that case. The charge can magnetize also, and electro magnet, like in a solenoid. Light can cause a chemical reaction, but a chemical reaction can also cause light, different light on the spectrum cause different reactions. Look at a glow fish, look at those things with the wheel with paper that is black on one side and white on the other that spin themselves, dark contracts, or absorbs, light reflects and emits, expands. Think of an eyeball, contracting and dilating... 
Now water acts a bit different, it expand with heat, and with freezing.
Now humans, we work the same way, only hybrid, we have magnetos, diesel, gas, internal combustion, and capacitors. That is how we fire neurons and move the solenoid we call muscles, but the eyes and skin are photo electric and chemical, and that chemical is melanin..
Sorry for rambling..

author: @musikSkool
text: We have some space heaters that say 1,500 watts on the box, but when I use a watt meter they never draw more than 400 watts.  Maybe after an hour or so they draw the full 1500, but I want to be warm now, not in an hour.  Are there brands of heaters that give full 1,500 watts the instant you plug them in?  I have seen several videos of people telling me 1500 watts is the same regardless of heater design, but those people are not taking into account how marketing works.  Corporations stretch the truth, 1500 watts is not 1500 watts if you graph it by average watts over time.  One brand might actually do 1500 watts average for an hour, while another brand may average 400 watts with the occasional 1500 watt peak that lasts a few seconds.

author: @StirlingLighthouse
text:
Great info. Thanks for clearing things up. 
I do have one rebut though. 
The advantage of a mass heater Vs a conventional wood stove is that way less of the heat is allowed to escape up the chimney. 
This reduces the amount of fuel burned by quite a lot. Some claim 1/10th of fuel.  Even if it 1/2 that means I don’t need to chop nearly as much wood. Win!  
Stay warm and merry Christmas to all. 🌲 🇨🇦 🌲

replies.author: @Paxmax
replies.text:
But... But then you are missing out the warmth and heat generated by chopping more wood!! Suuuurely that can't be effective?!?
Heehee!
Yeah, that 1/10 claim, if true at all, has alot of fudge factors people didn't- or forgot to mention. Usually when people get invested (me included) when heat tweaking we tend also to accept a lower heat standard after conversion... and many other improvements like mentioned; being better at closing door to less/unheated rooms. People tend to forget the other strategic changes, like centering more activities to the vicinity of RMHeaters. Before heat system overhaul, there might have been a 200°C hot iron stove that you really don't want to be close to because it gets unbearable at full throttle after 3 minutes... Where as a RMH has a more "tempered" behaviour.

I agree whole heartedly with your rebuttal, in an overwhelming majority of cases a Rocket stove puts out way more heat in the flue that a RMH is designed to capture.
Todays central gas-to-radiator water heater even capture the heat from condensation rather than letting that heat escape!! 
(Condensation clarification: When burning natural gas you essentially make alot of water vapor/steam aswell, steam being 100°C hot is forced to meet incoming cold-ish rad water, forcing the steam way over dew point so it condenses as water droplets. Just like it takes heat to make steam, steam gives off heat as it is changing phase from gas to liquid, and is suprising amounts of heat too, when you do calculations. Within context of a several 10's of kW gas burner heat it's a smallish number, I'd guess 2-4% or so, never done the calc's nor remember the ractual number, so grain of salt n all that)

If you have laminate, tiles or vinyl flooring the amount of fluff will surprise you! Do half the radiator and put your hand above the clean side you can feel the difference, obviously only with a hot radiator!!

author: @Guardian_Arias
text: I love how you have been deconstructing people's understanding of heating mechanisms, but why the over focus on filament and fuel based heating like electric heaters and rocket stoves? Heat pumps move 3 watts of heat per watt expended in many cases more. Plus there is the added benefit of cooling the home on hot days, because heat pumps do as they are named pump heat from one side to the other in either direction. Even a window unit located at the most lived in area ought to be more cost effective than electric heaters.

author: @totherarf
text:
I like to think of myself as a Contrarian ....... Awkward old git as my better half has been known to opine!

I would like to pick around some of the arguments you make, in the hope of further discussion .....
Not all Electric Heaters are equal or indeed as efficient as each other. Sure, energy in = energy out but we are talking about heat, not other energy!
If your heating element gets too hot it starts to glow ... that light is energy going out but not heat energy. Equally if your element is making a noise or is an induction element eather mechanical energy or magnetic energy is being exported rather than heat. If that other energy is converted to heat then all is good, but is it All converted?

To be sure these "losses" may be negligible, but they are there.
The converse is also true .... your 100 W lightbulb does use 100W of power but it does not give 100W of light out, as you soon realise when trying to touch one!

So "Efficiency" has both a literal and an implied meaning.
There is one type of electric heater though that is touted as being 400% efficient .... and it is!

A heat pump will give you 4 times more heat out of it than energy you put in, but it cheats!
The total energy of the device is slightly less than zero! You put energy in and the pump whirs to life and squirts heat out .... but at the expense of cooling the other side down (slightly more than it heats the other up). That is your second "Law" of thermodynamics in action!

author: @jasonburguess
text: Thought for the day: what if we used electrical plasma (like a plasma cutter 120-240vac input to 12-18vdc output) to heat water to boiling temperature and scavenged the steam pressure to recover electricity via a turbine, then ran the hot water through a radiant heat system installed in a floor to act as a condenser and then returned the water back to the boiler in a closed loop controlled by thermostatic valves and convection. Low input voltage causing a very high temperature boils water that then makes energy via turbine, then gives off almost all waste heat to thermal mass of a home that needs heat and the cycle begins again, extremely high efficiency could be achieved and the parts are all basically off the shelf pieces. Expensive initial install and construction, but the savings over time could be tremendous. Just a thought for the day.

replies.author: @Chaotyk-Official
replies.text: I don't think anything that uses a plasma cutter wud be efficient.   Need a more passive or low cost heat source

replies.author: @jasonburguess
replies.text: @Chaotyk-Official plasma cutter off the shelf runs about $200 air compressor required an additional $200 temperature achieved at the cutting tip can exceed 6 million degrees F. I have one and use it to cut steel regularly, if the cutter was focused on a steel container filled with water, the steel wouldn't burn through because of the water, like boiling water in a leaf over a fire, the water absorbs the heat so the leaf won't burn, another idea would be using induction in a steel container to heat the steel and thus the water as well, as long as the water is continuously supplied and allowed to boil before being replaced, steam could be produced making high pressures to send to a turbine and then to a condenser to release the heat to thermal mass, returning the steam to water, and circulating back to the boiler

replies.author: @Chaotyk-Official
replies.text: @jasonburguess I can tell what ur thinking. But the flaw is the expense of running the source. Also. The plamsa wud burn through even with water. Due to lidenfrost effect.

replies.author: @Chaotyk-Official
replies.text: Addressing ur other suggestion. A induction coil. Wud be more efficient than the plasma. But still expensive in terms of how much energy is used to heat the water. Burning a low cost fuel to heat the water is still more cost effective. The sheer wattage the coil and plasma require are the cause of the offset. Where as the stored energy in the low cost fuel coverts readily into energy

I live in LowerSaxoni/Germany Here the old Houses from 18.. have all really small rooms for better heating. Often the living room was behind the wall with the kitchen-oven so the heat from cooking heats up this room. Then the real old beds where like a wardrobe with doors so the body heat of the sleepers could heat this small space. 
In modern times not to follow this old rules became a status symbol.

author: @felipearbustopotd
text:
A sieve would probably retain more heat than the typical house could here in the UK... houses here are generally very leaky.  

If they could be hermetically sealed (ideally submarine tightness) then mechanically ventilated, I guess the need for excess energy to heat the room or human would be drastically reduced?

But as with all possible solutions, cost is a factor.

***
One piece shrink wrap plastic or even one piece bubble wrap over an internal window held in place with double sided tape works wonders for that extra layer of insulation and helps reduce the amount of condensation that would usually appear on the window pane.  

Pick a room, remove the furniture erect a gazebo / tent  (tall enough to move ard in) indoors, heat that space in comfort.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Technically correct, in practice given the fuel source is relatively the same, ie burning something not using electric heat or solar gain, mass heaters are more efficient as more of the heat is retained inside the structure by the simple expedient the exhaust gases are lower temperatures for the mass heater.  You can also run the fresh air supply for the heater (rocket stove) in through the exhaust pipe to scavenge even more heat.  3rdly, as you've said previously mass heaters give off a significant portion of their energy as radiant heat that heats you not the air which rises to the ceiling so less energy is required to achieve the same comfort level.  This leads to the next 1st principle that heat moves quicker when the temperature differential, the gradient if you will, is greater thus you lose heat faster without the mass because heat from such sources, thinking rocket stoves mainly here, fluctuates far more and in general you overheat the space for periods and when it cools you do it again in relatively short cycles.  Given rooms of the same size and insulative capacity you will find your fuel use significantly reduced overall with a mass heater.  Yes, early in the heating season it takes a bit more fuel to bring the mass's temperature up but once that is done you have reduced heat loss both by exhaust and by thermal losses through the rooms envelope, reduced fuel use to achieve the same level of comfort and the capacity to use short hot fires (complete combustion) increasing efficiency of converting fuel to heat.  Having years of practice in NE Ontario Canada where temperatures regularly reach -40C I can say that these considerations do indeed make a mass heater a better choice to use less fuel in a heating season unless you want to be tending the heater constantly and feeding it small portions of fuel every 5 minutes.  That doesn't even account for the huge $ savings for being able to use just about any biomass waste as fuel.  Similar constancy of comfort is currently achieved through electronic controls and condensing furnace technology while a RMH achieves that without most of the technological complexity so both it's efficiency and it's effectiveness are superior as heating units.  Much of this of course does not apply to non-burning tech such as electric heat or heat pumps.

I put it on a lanyard and it keeps my belly warm. I was wondering if you could find any improvements to the hand warmers? It would be quite the challenge but I feel that there some things that could be improved, like temperture control or alternative fuel sources.

I was just thinking about the possibility of building a used oil burning compression ignition engine, to work similar to the hot bulb diesel engines, when the diesel was first invented, I call it compression ignition engine because it's not really a diesel engine, and something like a 3.5" stroke, with a 4" bore, using another piston with something like a carburator, and spray the oil into the bore, with something like a 40mm bore, 50-60mm stroke, and maybe use a air compressor to blow air into the oil filled cylinder just as it starts to compress, and inject into the engines cylinder, starting at 80-120 psi, then compress all that volume into the nozzle area, 10cc or less, the fuel oil could possibly start to ignite inside the injector using the air to help spray the fuel/oil and possibly use a heat plug to get the engine running,   I'd really like to build a custom boxer engine, oil burner, with 15-30 psi boost, and run on waste engine or vegetable oil, with 6"-8" bore, and 12" stroke, having enough compression to start ignition when the piston is 1" down the bore, because the injector is supplying more air at high pressure, with 24:1-26:1 compression ratio, and inject when it's about 20:1,  the force will be much more efficient on the crank angle! The injector adding 50cc of air at 120psi before compressed and injection, it would require a few machined parts, repurposed parts from motor cycle or chain saw, maybe lawnmower engines, for building the injector,;  use a check valve in the nozzle with multiple injection points per nozzle on a large engine, possibly 12, 6, on small engines, the check valve should stay closed until an excess of 100-200 psi is reached,  working like a extra large p- pump on a Cummins diesel, that is connected directly to the head, by the nozzle, 
Either a boxer, or opposed piston 2 cylinder , basically diesel,  maybe a Subaru engine could be modified as a proof of concept, it's a kinda step backwards, to the first diesel engines,  it would probably require really thick oil, with additives to keep the film intact , with the high pressure involved,  with the extra atomized injections it could cut fuel consumption by half or more, maybe as much as 70%. And maybe it can run on liquid propane or natural gas! Maybe even gasoline,  with only a change in timing!

author: @DKFX1
text:
Very interesting thoughts. I will say however, I think it makes sense to talk about efficiency when it comes to combustible processes. Since for every substance, gasoline, diesel, wood, coal etc there is a specific output energy for a given amount of substance you input. By convention the amount of energy which can approximately be extracted of crude oil is 41868 joules per kilogram, for petrol it is 45800 joules. 

With a complete combustion about 8 kilowatt of heat can be produced from 1 kg coal. 1 kg of natural uranium after enrichment etc will produce 45000 KW of heat, which is actually far lower than other types of uranium

This is getting into theoretical physics but we also know that by the energy equivalence principle (the famous E=mc^2) that 1 kilogram of mass no matter its atomic constitution equates to 8.9x10^16 joules, and so we can put a conversion efficiency value on that relationship, even if we really wanted to know what type of pure mass-to-energy conversion method was the most efficient of all.

But I love our living room heater as it looks like a log burner with blue flames.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I would like to say, that, since I am possibly the one this is directed at [I could be wrong, but still there is a point to be made for who is saying something, and why], I wanted to clarify a couple of details when I personally use the term/phrase 'efficient'.

And I can speak for nobody other than myself when I say this.

As an energy researcher and engineer with a background in renewables and in turbine technology, I never use the word 'efficient' lightly, casually, or as a gimmick to sell something.

For me, efficiency is just short of a religion.

And when I say 'efficient', I say it as a systems engineer, not a bench test lab assistant.

In other words, the ENTIRE SYSTEM has to be efficient [or 'more' efficient, specifically] for me to say that, and this does not merely limit its self to the myopic, the extremely evident and the focused point, but includes the whole scope of factors.

These include draftiness, insulation, thermal exchange between materials, effort required to build, install and operate said system, and its projected over-time cost to maintain, and its expected life expectancy.

A great idea is only as great as the sum of its parts, after all.

If it runs wonderfully but only for one day, its really not a great idea.

If it pumps out a whole lot of heat, but that heat ends up not going where you need it, that's also a bad idea.

I realize that one of the biggest problems with combustion heat systems is that they consume oxygen, which leads to both a deprivation of breathable oxygen for the user in a poorly engineered system, as well as outputting carbon monoxide and dioxide.

The best known ways of preventing this is with a double displacement heat exchanged external forced air draft system. It is also known as 'regenerative preheating' and 'thermal scavenging.'

Admittedly, that can be quite pricey or challenging to build on one's own, especially if they are not handy with tools, although a lot of the more modern furnaces now come with them automatically.

Thermal scavenging, to me is the best term, because it really gets down to the point of what it is doing.

When you burn fuel, you will need air in this case, be it from inside, or from outside, and I get a lot of people saying that drawing in cold air is inefficient because it needs to be heated up more than internal air. In this case, that is 'technically' [again at bench test levels] true, however, it is not the end result of better heat usage to suck the warm air out and let cold air into your HOME to replace it, because this will prove to be FAR LESS efficient.

The thermal scavenging which is now commonly practiced in modern furnaces happens where the still warm exhaust which would otherwise be vented at less than an optimal level of thermal efficiency, is, instead, run through a heat exchanger where a lot of the remaining heat is given up to the incoming air.

This, in effect, keeps more of the developed heat inside the home, inside the system, where it is not a net loss.

Another issue is catalytic combustion.

I am a major proponent of the use of catalysts in combustion, simply because, under proper conditions, is proven to reach combustion efficiencies greater than 100% of adiabatic sealed caloric bomb style combustion , even at the best possible air/fuel mixtures.

If you can add the physics of velocity, pressure and turbulence, these efficiencies increase, because raising pressure reduces the energy required to maintain combustion, the velocity increases the energetic state of moving molecules, and turbulence increases propagation of combustion fronts through the air fuel mixture.

While actual physical combustion only occurs at the speed of sound, and in fact can not be made to go any faster, therefore there are a whole lot of limits on turbojet engines people do not know about [I've worked making jet engines for Rolls Royce, and this was a part of my job to study], but, there are 'cheats' to this, and they come mainly in the fact that while combustion its self only happens at the speed of sound, IGNITION can be initiated at about 80% the speed of light.

This is because the electrons involved in combustion are moving at roughly 80% the speed of light.

So, therefore, if one has a super heated containment of high velocity air and fuel being swirled in a regenerative chamber, the air and fuel can then be initiated into combustion faster than its theoretical velocity, and this allows for a thermal breakover point which is much like deflagration or detonation [in fact, its where NASA's 'new' (but actually not new at all) rotary detonation engine got its power from.

In essence, one can make a micro-rocket [a REAL 'rocket stove'] which puts out as much as 150% of the theoretical combustion energy in the fuel.

The issue then comes one of how to build it so it does not self destruct, I realize, but, with the right steel tubing and refractory materials, it can be done, and for not a whole lot of money.

Since physics are the same at all levels of scale, one can make this system rather small, and it will still perform like a larger version.

The only real drawback to a rotary detonation engine would be the noise, but if you plumb the exhaust properly, the sound can be self cancelled, effectively making it a self silenced system.

While that might be way outside of the scope of some of the people here, it is certainly a proven and doable thing.

While efficiency is a huge buzzword, its not just a buzzword, its more of a very poorly understood engineering term people cast about to make themselves sound intelligent when they are not.

That is why I do not use the word 'efficiency' lightly.

author: @salimufari
text:
While the principles you discus & teach are all very true there are improvements in how we use the available energy is where the water gets murky.
  A key part of the mass heater discussion is the energy collection. With fire you have said that some fire heat is by and large wasteful. The soot, wood gas & other fuel in the burn that usually go up the flue. The masonry heaters are the progenitors of the rocket stove movement. That in mind the point of masonry designs are all based on getting every joule of energy into the stone work in a very hot fast burn. 

  Another part of the "efficiency" argument is our effort put into tending the fire. A 2 hour fire heats your home for 24 hours involves so much less of our energy & fuel needed to get that space warm.

author: @malHHkenny
text:
The "comfort zone" quandary:

We can adopt another law: Energy naturally flows to misconception. ;) Much like heat, being well informed can be definitively managed. Preference/bias/superstition/sensation... not so much. Ignorance is cold bliss.

If we're not talking about loss, we're talking nonsense about efficiency. Inefficiency is loss. If we're talking about gaining efficiency without talking about reducing losses, we're talking nonsense. Any of us who think we can gain more than what we put in, are living in denial. If I'm being thoughtless and I don't feel like wearing warm clothing in small room with material construction that retains heat and staying close to a radiant heat source, then I am disregarding effectiveness. Any heat that I lose by being ineffective is by choice -- if I'm paying attention. Being well informed is also choice. That's what make this channel great: Continually more and more information about The Bigger Picture.

replies.text: That's how I feel about it as well. For those of us that use stoves it feels like we waste less heat using mass. I am applying 5lbs per week with a mix of modelers clay, sandstone chunks, 3-5 inch basalt stones to my wiseway pellet stove. Chestnut coal in the secondary burn chamber along with wood chunks allow me to stay very warm with using 40lbs of pellets per week. I'm watching every episode he does now, so glad he shares his knowledge and experience.

replies.author: @xanfsnark
replies.text:
Of course. All the mass does is change the speed at which the heat moves from the source to the destination. That's useful if (and only if) you have source of heat that is more efficient when it is inconveniently fast.

In the case of a biomass fire, it's more efficient to burn lean, fast, and hot, and to take up as much of the heat as possible, but heating a house that way would be briefly too hot, then too cold. The mass lets you burn fast but use the heat slow.

Similarly, a storage heater that can shift heat from a time when it's cheap to when it's more useful (e.g. nighttime electricity, or solar thermal during the day) is advantageous.

On the other hand, an efficient gas or oil burner can run well at lower outputs, so a mass heater powered by one of those wouldn't be as beneficial.

author: @comicmania2008
text: Very informative, thank you. With respect, you remind me much of a physics teacher I used to have at school back in the 1970s! I like your analogy of efficiency vs effectiveness, I'm retired now, was an electrician for most of my working life, and I currently use an 'oil filled' 1.5 KW rad type elec heater (with 3 settings) when I'm in my computer room (So max 1500W, mid 1000W, min 500W, it 'seems' the most effective heater out of all the heaters I ever tried. As long as the door and window to the room is shut, it keeps me nice and cosy! I initially blast it on 'max' for about half an hour, then run it at min 500W on half stat! This gets the room to a nice 24 DEG C. The stat then cuts in and out about every 5 minutes, 5 on 5 off, therefore only using 0.25 KWH when running at 24 DEG C. So works out at 13 pence an hour (EDF daytime rate)! I think being oil filled helps, no other elec heaters I tried to seem to work out that cheap. Merry Christmas to you Robert.

replies.text: in Belgium last week we had a bit of winter, temperatures in the day around the freezing point of water and frost in the night. 60Kwh electricity usage for 35 euro/day, I am heating a part of the house with infrared panels and one butane gas heater...  Even with many solar panels who produce 40Kwh/day in june or july on a sunny day, the costs are problematic.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Right now we are running a minimum of 6kw of heat trying to fight off this localized cold front.

To boost up the heat somewhat where we are trying to keep plants from freezing we are using another 5.6kw locally there to keep the plants from freezing.

author: @woolymittens
text: Canadian here - gas used to be quite cost effective but last year gas prices took a massive jump - I'm paying 50% more this year than last. its made electricity the more cost effective heating source. I'm running electric space heaters constantly during "off-peak" hours to reduce my gas consumption dramatically. My calculations show I'll save about 25% of what gas-only heating would cost.

replies.author: @EumlOriginal
replies.text:
You should be happy, here (Germany) the prices have exploded completely ...
We have always had very high prices (25 ct/kWh) but now we are at 53 ct/kWh and that will probably double again next year...

and before you get the wrong idea Electricity is even more expensive ...

replies.author: @MacGyverCanada
replies.text: Canadian here too.  Here in Nova Scotia there's not much hydroelectricity or natural gas.  Roughly half of our power is coal-generated, though there are big plans to change that soon.  We pay about $0.17/kWh, or we heat with furnace oil at $1.49/liter.

author: @tiitulitii
text: I am constantly wondering, if burning hydrogen generates more energy than what breaking it from water through electrolysis takes. Please, can you elaborate this?

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
Hello.
No.
Any energy transformation create loss, but if you produce too much with solar pannels or wind, instead of loosing everything, that energy can be transformed in hydrogen to be used later, when there is no sun, or wind.
The same idea as batteries electric, or sand, or mass heater, fly wheel, except hydrogen can be stored for more time, it could be produced during the sumer, and used during the winter, if the tank is big enough.
Cheers.

replies.author: @utopiavalonis
replies.text: @hommerdalor6301 and how can you get a tank of hydrogen + fuel cell for less money then traditional lithium batteries? That said, why can't we just buy a hydrogen stove on a hydrogen gas bottle, like you can have with butane gas now?

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
@utopiavalonis I saw someone on yt using empty butane/ propane bottles, compressing the hydrogen with a fridge compressor. The original stays in the bottle, so in case of leakage, we now.
The fuel cell can be diy.

author: @ifell3
text: Infrared heaters also produce light, so giving the lightbulb theory they produce less heat.

replies.author: @ifell3
replies.text: @justwords3882 not really, infrared heater provide light as well as heat. So it's not producing 100% heat, there is visible light produced too. Hence why I added the lightbulb. Old bulbs used to produce a lot of heat as well as light, the less heat the produce the more efficient they are. Opposite to the heater. If you had a heater that produced 50% light and 50 % heat of efficient is that HEATER.

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
If your heater produce light, you don’t need to use the normal light, so it’s still efficient.
;-)

author: @denisripley8699
text: Ah.. the corollary of 'warmth' is 'coolth'.  Cold windows and walls radiate 'coolth'.

replies.author: @joelaichner3025
replies.text: Ripley’s Believe it or not ! Are you Him ?

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
The cold window absorb your body heat, when the warm air comes in contact with the window, it becomes heavier, and falls down to the floor, its than flowing in the direction of the heat sources, your body, the heater wich makes the air lighter than it’s surroundings, and the cycle begins again. 
At the temperature of absolute 0 the molucules dont move, the warmer it gets, more they move, and bounce against each other, and the walls, people, when you go near a fire, you feel the vibration we call heat, near the cold window, the vibration is damped, less vibration = less heat.
Cheers.

replies.author: @Dr-Sy
replies.text: get a cup of cold water and place it on the side and see how long it takes to warm up? then take a cup of hot water place it on the side and see how long it takes to cool down. It also depends on where you put the cup such as in a freezer or on top of a hot stove..
